paragraphs.text: While it is very nice to see actions being taken in favor of digital fairness, I would like to bring the lawmakers' attention to one additional aspect in which consumer laws are being potentially violated - companies effectively destroying software, specifically video games. Currently many games require online access due to components not directly linked to the core product functionality, but rather to to additional functionalities like statistics gathering and digital goods marketplaces. When the companies publishing those games deem it no longer profitable, they're shutting down those online components, making the games unable to function, effectively destroying them. Some are even trying to force paying customers to destroy all copies of their bought product after the company decides that it no longer wants to support it through a one-sided and mandatory EULA. This problem is already being tackled by the 'Stop Destroying Videogames' European Citizens' Initiative, which has already gathered over 1.4 million signatures. Since the problem is quite complex, however, it'll be good to approach it from multiple different angles.
